comprehensive_summary:
Henrik Ibsen is a Norwegian playwright, widely considered the father of modern drama. His plays are central to quizbowl literature, with A Doll's House being by far his most frequently clued work.

A Doll's House shocked audiences by ending with protagonist Nora Helmer slamming the door on her marriage to the banker Torvald. Nora waits eight years for a 'wonderful thing' to happen before realizing she has never had a serious conversation with her husband. She tells Torvald 'I no longer believe in miracles.' Krogstad blackmails Nora over a forged signature. Doctor Rank, who suffers from 'tuberculosis of the spine' (widely held to be a euphemism for syphilis), confesses his love for Nora and helps her rehearse the Tarantella. Dr. Rank leaves visiting cards marked with a black cross to indicate his impending death. Nora sneaks in forbidden macaroons. A symbolic Christmas tree loses its decorations as Nora's marriage deteriorates. George Bernard Shaw wrote 'The Quintessence of Ibsenism' about Ibsen, analyzing his plays and critiquing Victorian ideals of the 'Womanly Woman.' Shaw's essay imagines a community of a thousand with '700 Philistines, 299 Idealists, and only one lone realist.' August Strindberg wrote The Father as a response, arguing Nora is the true villain. Adrienne Rich's essay 'When We Dead Awaken: Writing as Re-Vision' takes its title from Ibsen. Lu Xun gave a 1923 speech arguing Nora will likely 'fall into degradation.' Elfriede Jelinek's first play imagines the fate of one of Ibsen's characters. Ibsen called an alternate ending where Nora shouts 'Motherless!' 'an act of barbaric violence.'

Hedda Gabler: the title character burns Eilert Lovborg's manuscript and is blackmailed by Judge Brack. She imagines her lover dying with 'vine-leaves in his hair,' but he actually dies in a brothel run by Mademoiselle Diana. The play ends with the exclamation 'Good God! -- people don't do such things.' Hedda plays a 'wild dance' on the piano and shoots herself with one of her father's pistols.

The Wild Duck: Gregers Werle convinces the short-sighted Hedvig to sacrifice the title animal, but she shoots herself instead. Dr. Relling calls illusion 'the stimulating principle' and introduces the concept of the 'life-lie.' Hjalmar Ekdal's 'Great Invention' in photography is revealed to be a life-lie. Hjalmar's father Old Ekdal lives in an attic. Hjalmar's wife Gina admits an affair with Hakon Werle. Gregers mocks his father's efforts to reconnect as a 'tableau of filial affection' for his mistress Mrs. Sorby. Alfred Adler's work on the 'life-lie' was inspired by this play.

The Master Builder: Hilda Wangel convinces the acrophobic architect Halvard Solness to climb a church steeple, from which he falls to his death. Hilda travels from Lysanger to remind him of his promise to build her a kingdom. Solness fears the 'younger generation' knocking at his door and wishes a chimney crack would burn down his wife's house.

An Enemy of the People: Dr. Thomas Stockmann discovers that the water at a newly opened spa is contaminated and gives a speech denouncing the 'compact majority.' He compares a political party to a 'sausage machine.' Mrs. Busk reluctantly fires Stockmann's daughter. The play ends with a declaration about 'the spirit of truth and the spirit of freedom.'

Ghosts: Mrs. Alving dedicates an orphanage in memory of her late husband. The orphanage is left uninsured and burns down. Her son Oswald has syphilis and mutters 'the sun, the sun' in the final scene as his mother decides whether to euthanize him. Pinero reconsidered his own work after seeing Ghosts.

When We Dead Awaken is Ibsen's final play. Irene sings 'I am free as a bird!' and calls posing for the sculpture Resurrection 'a self-murder.' She and sculptor Arnold Rubek are buried by an avalanche. A Sister of Mercy speaks the final line 'Pax vobiscum!' The play includes a speech about how after the title action, 'we find that we have never lived.'

Other works: Pillars of Society features Consul Bernick and the dangerously unseaworthy Indian Girl. John Gabriel Borkman features a banker living in his attic since imprisonment. The Lady from the Sea features Ellida, married to a doctor, haunted by a former American sailor husband. Little Eyolf features the Rat-Wife and a boy's death, leaving a floating crutch. Peer Gynt features a funeral and auction. Rosmersholm is discussed by Strindberg as 'psychic murder.' Brian Johnston argued Ibsen's final twelve plays form a single Hegelian framework. William Archer and Edmund Gosse popularized Ibsen in Britain. Ibsen characterized Norwegian literature after the sagas as 'four hundred years of darkness.' He coined the Scandinavian ideal of 'Friluftsliv' (free air living). Arthur Miller compared his plays to a 'spiritual CAT scan.'

works.name: A Doll's House
works.description: Nora Helmer slams the door on her marriage to Torvald, shocking audiences. Features Krogstad's blackmail, Doctor Rank's illness, and the Tarantella rehearsal.

clues.clue: Ends with Nora slamming the door on her marriage to Torvald
quotes: shocked audiences by ending with its main character slamming the door
this sound can be heard offstage after a 'hope inspires' a character to mutter 'the miracle of miracles-?'

quotes: A character created by this author waits eight years for a 'wonderful thing' to happen

quotes: Doctor Rank confesses his love for a woman whom he helps rehearse the Tarantella, and Krogstad blackmails Nora Helmer

quotes: 'tuberculosis of the spine' afflicts Dr. Rank

clues.clue: Nora sneaks in forbidden macaroons
quotes: Nora demonstrates her independence by sneaking in some of these items that were forbidden by her husband

clues.clue: Symbolic Christmas tree loses decorations as marriage deteriorates
quotes: a symbolic Christmas tree loses its decorations and its vitality as a woman's marriage to a banker deteriorates

quotes: this author called an alternate ending to one of his plays where the protagonist shouts, 'Motherless!' and sinks to the floor 'an act of barbaric violence'

clues.clue: Inspired Lu Xun's 1923 speech that Nora will 'fall into degradation'
quotes: One of this author's characters will likely 'fall into degradation,' according to a 1923 speech by Lu Xun

quotes: One of these authors wrote The Father as a response to a play by the other, arguing that Nora, and not Torvald, is the true villain

quotes: a play whose title character uses Judge Brack's two pistols to shoot herself

works.name: Other Plays
works.description: Various other plays including Pillars of Society, John Gabriel Borkman, The Lady from the Sea, Little Eyolf, Peer Gynt, and Rosmersholm.

clues.clue: Pillars of Society: Consul Bernick pushes the unseaworthy Indian Girl to sail; his son stows away on it
quotes: The main character's young son stows away on the dangerously unseaworthy ship the Indian Girl

clues.clue: John Gabriel Borkman: banker lives in attic since imprisonment for embezzlement; son cries 'I don't want to work! I just want to live!'
quotes: The title character of a play by this man is a banker who has lived in his attic since his imprisonment for embezzlement

clues.clue: Little Eyolf: a boy dies following a Rat-Wife into the sea; a floating crutch is left behind; Rita hears 'the crutch is floating'
quotes: After a boy dies by following a Rat-Wife into the sea, only one of these symbolic objects is left behind

clues.clue: The Lady from the Sea: Ellida is haunted by a former American sailor husband who fled after murder
quotes: his wife is frightened by imagining a sailor reproaching his beloved for infidelity

clues.clue: Rosmersholm: Strindberg called Rebecca West an 'unconscious cannibal' in 'Psychic Murder (Apropos Rosmersholm)'
quotes: This author called Rebecca West an 'unconscious cannibal, who has devoured Mrs. Rosmer's soul'

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Norwegian playwright considered the father of modern drama. Shaw wrote 'The Quintessence of Ibsenism.' Bitter rivalry with Strindberg. Popularized in Britain by William Archer and Edmund Gosse.

clues.clue: Shaw wrote 'The Quintessence of Ibsenism': '700 Philistines, 299 Idealists, and only one lone realist'
quotes: George Bernard Shaw wrote a long essay about the 'quintessence' of this playwright
For every thousand of this author's characters, there are '700 Philistines, 299 Idealists, and only one lone realist'

clues.clue: Bitter one-sided rivalry with August Strindberg
quotes: This author's largely one-sided rivalry with August Strindberg
One of these authors kept a portrait of the other in his study and called it 'Madness Incipient'

clues.clue: Popularized in Britain by William Archer and Edmund Gosse
quotes: William Archer and Edmund Gosse were largely responsible for popularizing this author in the English-speaking world

clues.clue: Arthur Miller compared his plays to a 'spiritual CAT scan'
quotes: Arthur Miller compared this man's plays to a 'spiritual CAT scan'

clues.clue: Brian Johnston argued final twelve plays form a single Hegelian framework
quotes: Brian Johnston wrote that this author's final twelve plays constituted a single Hegelian framework

clues.clue: Characterized Norwegian literature after the sagas as 'four hundred years of darkness'
quotes: This author characterized Norwegian literature after the sagas as 'four hundred years of darkness'

clues.clue: Coined the ideal of 'Friluftsliv' (free air living)
quotes: A 19th-century author from this multinational region coined its ideal of 'free air living'

clues.clue: Jeanette Lee wrote about 'Ibsen's Secret' -- plays based around central symbolic objects
quotes: Jeanette Lee wrote a book titled for this author's 'Secret' in which she claimed that this author's plays are based around central symbolic objects
